column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Titanium Alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/alloy/titanium-alloy-ti-6al-4v-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for metal oxide removal
Near-IR wavelength for optimal metal absorption
Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution
Energy per unit area for oxide removal
Pulse duration for minimal heat-affected zone
Beam travel speed for uniform treatment
Number of cleaning cycles for complete removal

Typical range for 1064 nm ns-pulsed lasers in industrial cleaning of Ti-6Al-4V to remove contaminants without excessive heating; starting at 100 W for light oxidation, max limited by low thermal conductivity (6.7 W/m·K) to avoid melting (1660°C); source: CleanLaser CL 1000 manual and study in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on Ti alloys.

Effective range for ablation of surface contaminants; 2000 mm/s recommended for optimal balance of removal efficiency and minimal HAZ on reflective Ti-6Al-4V; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination like oxides, faster for light residues; source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment guidelines and Journal of Laser Applications (2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning; 20 ns recommended to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm) on heat-sensitive Ti-6Al-4V, avoiding picosecond/femtosecond unless precision required (which increase cost); ns sufficient for contamination removal without plasma shielding issues; source: Laser physics in Applied Surface Science (2019) on ablation thresholds for Ti alloys.

Typical for pulsed cleaning to control energy delivery; 30 kHz recommended for Ti-6Al-4V to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation due to moderate reflectivity (0.4 at 1064 nm); higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up but risk thermal buildup; source: Academic literature in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021).

Fluence range for safe contaminant ablation; damage threshold ~1.5-2 J/cm² for ns pulses on Ti-6Al-4V, recommended 1.0 J/cm² for cleaning without substrate melting or oxidation; conservative due to low thermal diffusivity; source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017) and Optik (2022).

Beam diameter range for focused cleaning; 200 μm recommended for Ti-6Al-4V surface preparation to balance resolution and coverage, smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas; ensures uniform fluence on reflective surfaces; source: Equipment specs from IPG Photonics YLR series and Surface and Coatings Technology (2019).

Single pass often sufficient for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on Ti-6Al-4V without over-ablation, allowing cooling between passes; source: Industry case studies in Proceedings of SPIE (2020) on laser cleaning of aerospace Ti alloys.

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or excessive overlap heating; 60% recommended for Ti-6Al-4V to achieve even cleaning on curved or irregular surfaces, higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination; source: Process optimization in International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (2018).
